title: Indigo vat dyeing basics
subtitle: Setting up and maintaining a fructose-lime reduction vat for blue
excerpt: Indigo is insoluble in water and cannot be used as a standard dye bath. It must be reduced to its soluble, colourless form in an alkaline vat, then reoxidised on the fibre to become permanent blue. The fructose-lime vat is a reliable, low-toxicity method suitable for home use. No mordant is needed.
sourceNotes: Indigo reduction chemistry and the fructose-lime (iron-free) vat method are documented in natural dyeing texts including Michel Garcia's vat documentation and Jenny Dean's 'Wild Colour'. The chemistry of leuco-indigo and oxidation on fibre is standard. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: An alkaline, oxygen-depleted solution in which insoluble indigo pigment is converted to its soluble yellow-green 'leuco' form. Fibre dipped into the vat absorbs leuco-indigo, which oxidises to blue on contact with air.
The soluble, yellow-green reduced form of indigo that forms inside the vat. Leuco-indigo absorbs onto fibre while it is in the vat; it turns blue when the fibre is removed and exposed to oxygen.

The colour-development stage of indigo dyeing: as the fibre is removed from the vat and exposed to air, leuco-indigo oxidises and reverts to the permanent blue insoluble pigment locked into the fibre.
The ongoing process of keeping the indigo vat reduced and active by adding small amounts of reducing agent and alkali when needed. A well-maintained vat can be used for days or weeks.

content.text: : an alkaline, oxygen-free environment where the insoluble pigment converts to its soluble
form. When fibre is removed from the vat,
turns the leuco-indigo back to blue, permanently bound to the fibre. No mordant is needed: indigo does not require a mordant to bond to protein fibres.

attrs.title: Alkali and gloves
attrs.body: Slaked lime is strongly alkaline. Wear gloves throughout and wash any skin contact immediately in cold water.

items.name: Fructose (fruit sugar, from supermarket or pharmacy)
Stainless steel or plastic container, minimum 8 litre
Fibre: wool, silk, or cotton (pre-wetted, no mordant needed)

content.text: In a separate cup, mix the indigo powder with a small amount of warm water to form a smooth paste. In another cup, dissolve the fructose in a little warm water. In the main container, add 5 litres of warm water (30-35°C). Carefully stir in the slaked lime, then the fructose solution, then the indigo paste. Stir very gently in a circle without splashing: introducing air at this stage will consume the reducing agent. Cover the vat and leave undisturbed for 30-60 minutes.
Step 2: Check the vat is ready

A ready vat looks yellow-green to olive-green below the surface, with a blue-purple surface film (the flower). Gently press the film aside and look at the liquid: it should be translucent yellow-green, not opaque blue. Check the pH with strips: the vat should be pH 9-11. Dip a white fabric scrap briefly into the vat: it should come out yellow-green and turn blue within 30 seconds of air exposure. If it comes out blue in the vat, the reduction is incomplete.

Lower the pre-wetted fibre slowly into the vat, avoiding splashing. Move gently in the vat for 2-3 minutes, then lift slowly and allow to drip back into the vat. Hang the fibre in air for 10-15 minutes, watching it turn from yellow-green to blue. Each dip adds one layer of colour. Repeat for deeper blues: 3-4 dips gives a medium blue; 8-10 dips gives a deep indigo.

After the final dip, rinse the fibre in warm water until the rinse runs clear. Add a small splash of white vinegar to the final rinse (enough to slightly acidify) to neutralise residual lime. Dry in the air.
: a well-built vat can be reactivated the following day by gently warming to 30-35°C and adding a small amount of additional fructose and lime if the pH has dropped below 9.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems in indigo vat dyeing.

items.symptom: Fibre comes out blue in the vat rather than yellow-green
items.cause: The vat is not reduced: the indigo has not been converted to leuco-indigo. The fructose reduction may need more time or the pH may be too low.
items.fix: Cover the vat and wait another 30-60 minutes. Check pH and add a small amount of lime if below 9. Do not dye until the vat colour is yellow-green below the surface.

items.symptom: Blue turns patchy or rubbed off when dry
items.cause: The indigo did not fully oxidise between dips, or the vat was too concentrated for the number of dips used.
items.fix: Allow full oxidation time (15 minutes minimum) between each dip. A very dark, heavy indigo deposit from too few dips in a very strong vat can feel powdery and rub off: multiple shorter dips in a weaker vat gives more durable colour.
